data.title: Uber: This Makes No Sense
data.description:
Thesis Summary

In my last article, I argued that Uber (UBER) was way too cheap. Since then, the stock has fallen roughly 15%.

So what has changed?

Fundamentally, very little. If anything, the outlook for business has improved after Q4.

What has changed is the narrative. There are some justified concerns around AI and competition.

However, the concerns are overblown, at least as far as they relate to Uber.

Uber should not be put into the same box as software, since its true value comes from its network.

Q4 confirmed the core business is accelerating, margins are durable, and free cash flow is compounding. And it’s just going to get better with full autonomy.

With Uber stock just above its 200-week moving average, I would strongly urge investors to consider adding to or starting a position here.

Q4 Results: All Is Well

Let’s start by looking at the latest numbers.

Revenue came in at $14.37B, a healthy 20% YoY increase.

Meanwhile, gross bookings grew 22%, with monthly active platform consumers crossing over 200 million.

For the full year, adjusted EBITDA was $8.7B, a +35% YoY increase, and free cash flow was just shy of $10B, up 42% YoY.

There’s just no denying now that Uber is generating real cash and value at scale.

While rides are still the bulk of the business, deliveries continued to surprise, with accelerating growth driven by grocery, retail, and expansion into less dense markets.

And what’s more, insurance, once the structural headwind, is turning into a tailwind.

If you step back from the noise, the business is functioning exactly as you’d want it to. And yet, the stock is down 30% since it crossed over $100.

AI Risk? I Don’t See It

The only reasonable explanation here is that the market has its doubts over AI and autonomy.

Some investors believe that autonomous vehicles, which are capital-intensive, could potentially compress Uber’s margins, or worse yet, displace the company completely.

But that misunderstands Uber’s positioning.

First, Uber is not trying to win the AV technology race.

It’s positioning itself as the demand layer.

The CEO, Dara Khosrowshahi, was explicit about this in the Q4 investor letter.

Utilization on Uber’s platform is already structurally higher than standalone deployments.

Trips per vehicle per day are reportedly over 30% higher when integrated into Uber’s network.

In short, demand density matters.

Secondly, 70% of U.S. profits come from markets outside the top 20 cities.

AV deployments will be concentrated in dense urban cores for years.

International mobility represents 60% of gross bookings. The idea that autonomy suddenly replaces Uber’s profit pool is unrealistic, at least in the medium term.

And thirdly, capital intensity fears are overblown.

Even if AVs become the new norm, Uber does not need to own vehicles to participate in the upside. The company is already striking supply agreements and minority investments, not building factories.

Most importantly, AVs are a tailwind, as proven by early data from San Francisco and Austin, which show gross bookings accelerating in markets where AV presence increased.

AI strengthens Uber.

It optimizes the company and highlights the business's true value of its network. This is one thing AI and competitors won’t be able to replace, and it’s Uber’s ultimate moat.

Valuation Makes No Sense

As it stands right now, Uber trades at:

  • 21x forward P/E non-GAAP

  • 2.5x forward EV/sales

  • 13x forward EV/EBITDA

  • Forward PEG below 1

  • Price/Cash Flow below 15

This, at a time when free cash flow is growing 40%, EBITDA is growing in the mid-30s, and audience growth is accelerating.

The market is pricing Uber like it’s under threat or like its growth prospects are small, and yet, that’s far from the case.

Penetration remains low.

According to management, only around 15% of adults in top markets use the platform, and cross-product penetration remains around 20%.

There is plenty of room to grow, and Uber’s already printing $10 billion in cash flow per year.

Technical Analysis

If fundamentals alone don’t convince you, just have a look at the technical chart.

The stock is hovering just above the 200-week EMA, an ideal support area.

RSI has cooled into the lower range, almost oversold, and this gives me some confidence that a reversal could be near.

If we do break lower, there is a strong volume support around the $55 area.

However, at current levels, risk-reward appears skewed to the upside, in my opinion.

Risks

Although I remain very bullish on Uber, there are some potential risk factors.

While AVs are an opportunity, they are also a threat, and big companies like Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG) and Tesla (TSLA) are working hard to dominate this market.

Secondly, regulation could also become an obstacle to Uber, be it in terms of AV or laws that regulate Uber's relationship with its drivers.

Thirdly, there's still room for Uber's share price to keep sliding if the software sell-off continues. There's still no clear signal of a reversal, and this is still a falling knife.

Final Thoughts

Uber has been caught in the software sell-off when it is arguably going to be a strong beneficiary of AI and other technological improvements.

The company is not under any threat from being replaced by AI vibe-coded competitors, and it commands one of the most valuable platforms in the world.

I think buying Uber here is a pretty easy decision.

data.title: Itron: Despite A Cheap Relative Valuation, Shares Don't Justify An Upgrade Now
data.description:
February 17th was a really great day for shareholders of Itron, Inc. (ITRI). After management announced financial results for the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year, shares jumped, closing up 7.9%. Unfortunately, revenue did decline year over year. But net profits and adjusted net profits both rose nicely, while cash flows followed suit. This is great to see in and of itself, even though the drop in sales is discouraging.

In the past, I have taken a more neutral stance on the company. In fact, in my last article about it, published a little over three years ago now, I called it a Hold candidate because of uncertainty. Revenue and cash flow at that time were disappointing. And the stock was not cheap enough to justify a bullish outlook.

Since that time, the stock has risen 72.6%. This almost perfectly matches the 74% increase that the S&P 500 (SP500) enjoyed. Considering that I view a Hold candidate as one that should perform along the lines of the broader market for the foreseeable future, I would say that this is a win. Looking at the picture today, I do still see some uncertainty. That's especially true when it comes to profitability for the year. Although the business ended 2025 on a strong note, the expectation that management has is for profits to fall this year. The good news is that the stock is priced at lower levels than other similar firms. But even with that factored in, I am not ready to take a bullish stance on the business just yet.

A Great Quarter

At first glance, the final quarter of the 2025 fiscal year might not have seemed all that great for shareholders of Itron. Take revenue as an example. During the quarter, revenue for the company came in at $571.7 million. That's a decline of 6.7% compared to the $612.9 million the business reported a year earlier. Even though this drop was discouraging to see, one positive thing was that revenue actually came in above what analysts were hoping for. They thought that sales would be $10.1 million lower than what they ended up being. The good news is that this was not the only area of strength for the business.

As an example, we need only look at the bottom line. Earnings per share jumped from $1.26 to $2.21. That is a monumental move higher, and it translated to a rise in net profits from $58.1 million to $101.6 million. The earnings per share reported by the company ended up being $0.17 greater than what analysts were hoping for. On an adjusted basis, the picture was even better, with a rise in earnings from $1.35 to $2.46. That ended up exceeding analysts’ estimates by $0.27 per share and meant a rise in adjusted net income from $62.2 million to $113.4 million.

Other profitability metrics for the company performed well also. Operating cash flow expanded from $79.8 million to $119.3 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a near doubling from $75.2 million to $150.1 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $81.4 million to $98.8 million.

The chart above shows financial performance for the business for the last three completed fiscal years now. Unfortunately, 2025 in its entirety was a time of weakness on the top line. But on the bottom line, the company has continued to improve from one year to the next. Much of this improvement came from an expansion of the company's gross profit margin. Even as revenue dropped from 2024 to 2025, for instance, gross profit expanded from $839.3 million to $892.1 million. This meant a rise in gross profit margin from 34.4% to 37.7%, with management attributing this to a favorable change between product and solution mix and manufacturing efficiencies that the company has captured. Overall operating costs did increase, but that was not enough to offset the improvements on the gross profit side of things.

In the chart above, you can see that, in recent years, the company’s smallest segment, known as Outcomes, has done exceptionally well. This part of the company offers up certain software and services aimed at helping users to manage, organize, analyze, and interpret, raw and anonymized data through the use of AI, machine learning, and other analytics-related methods. Customers such as municipalities, utilities, and others, are the primary target here. The ultimate goal is to use software to massively improve operational quality. And clearly, the services that the company has here are working. Over the last few years, revenue and profits have risen nicely.

From 2024 to 2025, for instance, the company saw significant improvement, with management chalking this up to greater recurring revenue and a rise in demand for the professional services and hardware that it offers. The great thing about software and service-oriented operations is that they have a tendency to have high margins and the ability to scale profitability faster than revenue. Sure enough, that is exactly what we have seen. The segment profit margin back in 2023 was only 18.8%. But by 2025, that number had grown to 21.4%.

This has not been the only area of growth for the company. Although I guess it would be appropriate to say it's the only area of growth recently. The Networked Solutions business has, as the chart above illustrates, been quite lumpy when it comes to revenue. But one thing that I will point out is that overall profitability has been rising consistently. This is the part of the company, for context, that offers things like smart meters, modules, points, and sensors, as well as certain network infrastructure, network design services, and more, all dedicated to the companies and the electricity, water, gas, and other end markets.

Even as revenue has started dropping from 2024 through the present day, profits have risen. Management has blamed the decline in revenue lately on a change in the timing of customer deployments and a surge in volume that was seen in the first half of 2024 that has since slowed down. That robust demand back then, according to the company, was thanks to customers having to catch up following supply chain constraints caused by the COVID-19 pandemic. So honestly, the recent decline here does make sense. But still, profitability was able to increase because of a favorable change in customer mix.

The only other operating segment that is really worth going over is the Device Solutions segment. Through this unit, the company offers up hardware products that are used for the measurement, control, and sensing, for various industries like electricity, gas, and water. Revenue here has been dropping since 2024 as well. And based on what management has said, that is primarily because of a decrease in electric residential sales, most notably throughout Europe, the Middle East, and Africa. Even though the business benefited from higher smart water shipments, water meter sales also declined from 2024 to 2025. But once again, improvements on the bottom line were noteworthy. According to management, the business continues to experience an improvement in customer and product mix. And this specifically stems from the end-of-life of some of its lower-margin products.

Another thing that investors should be aware of when it comes to the business is that management is very interested in growing by means of acquisition. The firm recently acquired, for instance, Urbint. This was a privately held software company that utilizes real-world data and artificial intelligence in order to help customers to prevent incidents that threaten critical infrastructure, workers, the environment, and members of the community. This was not a cheap purchase, costing the company $325 million. Then, only a couple of weeks after that, the company announced its acquisition of Locusview, which is a utility focused software and services company with operations in the U.S. and Israel. Its emphasis is on digital construction management solutions. This deal actually closed subsequent to the end of the quarter, costing shareholders $525 million.

Normally, I would expect acquisitions like this to immediately boost revenue. But unfortunately, when it comes to 2026, management expects sales to only be between $2.35 billion and $2.45 billion. Last year, for instance, revenue was $2.37 billion. So we really aren't moving the needle here. At the same time, the company expects adjusted earnings per share to come in at between $5.75 and $6.25. At the midpoint, this would mean adjusted net profits of $276.5 million, which would be down from the $330.4 million the company reported for 2025. About $17.5 million of this profit decrease can be attributed to additional interest expense caused by the aforementioned acquisitions. But still, this doesn't change the fact that profitability is worsening. Unfortunately, this likely means adjusted operating cash flow of about $417 million and EBITDA of around $312.8 million.

If we use these estimates provided by management, and we also use the results that the company achieved for 2025, we can see in the chart below how shares are currently priced. But that's not all. You can also see how the company is priced using estimates for next year. This is based on management's guidance, shown above, for revenue of between $2.60 billion and $2.80 billion. The EBITDA margin for the company of between 15% and 17% implies EBITDA at the midpoint of $432 million. And that would likely mean adjusted net profits of $381.8 million, as well as adjusted operating cash flow of $574.5 million.

This is fine, but it's not really fantastic. Of course, relative to other companies that have similarities to it, the stock actually looks relatively attractive. In the chart below, I compared it to five such businesses. On an EV-to-EBITDA basis, only one of the companies that I stacked it up against happens to be cheaper than it is. But when it comes to the other two valuation approaches, our candidate is the cheapest of the group.

Takeaway

Operationally speaking, I would consider Itron to be an interesting company. But it's not one that I can get behind just yet. I am encouraged by management's expectations for the next couple of years. But even with that factored in, I wouldn't say that this is a value play. In fact, I would argue that it makes for a much better Hold candidate than it does a Buy candidate right now.

data.title: DTCR: Real Estate Emerges As The New Hot AI Play
data.description:
Has AI turned heel? So far this month, software-as-a-service stocks have become more like “dis-service” names. Their once stalwart high-margin, high-ARR business models are quickly being rethought as AI further infiltrates the corporate enterprise landscape.

But one area not so downstream of AI is the physical data center space. Back in September, I noted potential bullish price action in the Global X Data Center & Digital Infrastructure ETF (DTCR). Holding a more than 20-percentage-point overweight to Real Estate compared to the S&P 500, this AI strategy has shot higher, returning 31% since my Q3 2025 analysis.

I reiterate a "Buy" rating today. I continue to see green shoots in Real Estate, which should help DTCR outperform now-lagging tech niche funds. The valuation remains compelling, while the technicals point to the bulls maintaining control.

DTCR Beating Tech & AI ETFs YoY

According to the issuer, DTCR seeks to track the Solactive Data Center REITs & Digital Infrastructure Index and aims to "invest in companies that operate in data centers and other digital infrastructure supporting the growth of communication networks." Digital infrastructure spans multiple segments, with innovative companies ranging from household names to newcomers.

DTCR is a fast-growing ETF, now with $1.16 billion in assets under management as of Feb. 13, 2026. That’s up from just $438 million at the time of my previous assessment. The fund’s annual expense ratio is moderate at 50 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at only 88 basis points (about 0.3 percentage points less than that of the S&P 500).

Share-price momentum is best in class, earning DTCR a pristine A+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. The portfolio is not without risk, however, as its historical realized standard deviation is lofty, and I’ll point out later that the product has a notorious history of bouncing quickly between overbought and oversold conditions.

Still, DTCR is nicely tradeable—average daily volume has gone straight up since last September, now more than 600,000 shares. The median 30-day bid/ask spread, meanwhile, is somewhat tight at 8 basis points, per Global X ETFs.

For an update on the portfolio, the 5-star, Negative-rated ETF by Morningstar has mixed exposure across the style box. The plurality of the holdings—just 28%--is found in the mid-cap blend style box. There’s about an even balance between value, blend, and growth. What’s more, ample size scattering helps from a diversification point of view. I’d call out that there’s 27% allocated to ex-U.S. stocks—a clear alpha driver over the past year.

On valuation, DTCR remains very compelling. The price-to-earnings ratio is low at only 14.4x, while the long-term earnings growth rate is decent at 9.8%. The resulting PEG ratio is a solid 1.5x.

DTCR: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Looking closer at the sector breakdown, DTCR is primarily an Information Technology and Real Estate play. That's a nice growth-value offset. Of course, it is cap weighted, so paying attention to both the fundamentals and technicals of the largest handful of positions is key.

DTCR: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, there’s not much to be gleaned from DTCR’s brief history. I also scanned Real Estate calendar trends, and they actually appear similar to DTCR’s. So, now through May does not significantly lean bullish or bearish from the singular seasonality standpoint.

DTCR: Neutral Trends Through May

The Technical Take

With intense momentum and an attractive valuation, DTCR’s technical situation is strong. Notice in the chart below that shares printed all-time highs on the morning of Feb. 17, 2026. While sellers came about later in the day, the broader trend is higher. I make that assertion based on the rising long-term 200-day moving average and the 50 DMA, which is above the 200 DMA (with price above both trend-indicator lines).

Also take a look at the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph. It may be cooling off from a period of high volatility, within a bullish range of 50-80. But, as mentioned before, DTCR has a history of dropping fast, leading to oversold RSI readings.

For now, I see support at the October high just below the $23 mark (that’s where the ETF retreated to recently), while long-term support is down between $19 and $20. A high amount of shares traded in the low $20s suggests that zone should be ample support.

DTCR: Bullish Uptrend, $23 Support, Solid RSI

The Bottom Line

I have a "Buy" rating on DTCR. The fund has outperformed the Information Technology sector since October, all while software AI names have come under attack. With a low valuation and strong momentum, I expect higher prices this year.

data.title: InMode: Some Green Shoots Appearing, But The Market Hasn't Noticed
data.description:
InMode Ltd. (INMD) is an Israeli-based company in the medical aesthetics space, offering platforms and technology to serve both minimally invasive and non-invasive procedures. They offer 12 platforms (machines) offering a range of treatments involving radio-frequency (RF) micro-needling technology in the areas of plastic surgery, gynecology, dermatology, otolaryngology, and ophthalmology. Probably the most well-known are Morpheus8, Optimas MAX, and IgniteRF, shown below.

The company serves an important niche market between laser procedures and plastic surgery, providing patients comparable results to the former but without the anesthesia, downtime, and scars. It is important to note that InMode now offers some laser-based treatments, but these are not their main “bread and butter,” as they aim to fill the “treatment gap,” as seen below.

There has been some interesting news coming from this company in recent weeks, and I’d like to provide an update in the context of their recently released Q4 and full-year results. I have covered InMode on two previous occasions and suggested a “Strong Buy” rating. My initial deep dive can be found >>here<< while my most recent reiteration (from December 2025) can be read >>here<<. I would suggest readers look at these articles first to get a good sense of my background as both an analyst and investor in this company.

My most recent article generated over 100 comments, which suggests there remains ongoing debate about the company and its potential going forward, as an entity in itself and as an investment opportunity. I must say that I found the dialogue with readers in my previous two articles insightful and very rewarding, and I have endeavored on this occasion to incorporate some of the learnings from these interactions here. Indeed, as a Seeking Alpha analyst, I consider researching for the purposes of writing on the subject just half of the learning process; the other half comes from engaging with readers who can offer different viewpoints or highlight new things for me to understand. I encourage and welcome the same respectful interactions for this article too.

For almost a year, INMD shares have treaded water between $13 and $16. They reached a low of just over $13 in June last year and, caught between hefty stock buybacks and stagnating sentiment, shares remain “stuck” without an obvious catalyst in sight. Bulls have pointed to InMode’s superb balance sheet (no debt, 60% of market cap in cash), sustained FCF generation, strong market position, market-leading proprietary technology, and new platform innovations, while bears have highlighted revenue declines, falling margins, perceptibly ineffective management, a weakening consumer, and weak macro generally, amidst geopolitical instability in Israel, as ongoing investment risks.

In my last coverage I also highlighted some company-specific issues related to what I described as ‘major deficiencies in their (especially U.S.) sales department’. A reader also highlighted to me several reports compiled in relation to sexual harassment at the company, among its sales staff, found here and here. I suggest readers take a look themselves at the allegations and assess InMode’s response. For reference, their new President of North American sales, Michael Dennison, began work in his new role during Q4, so investors will have to wait to see how quickly he can change things around. Internal cultural change takes time, particularly for a sales team on the road and in a different country from where the CEO and upper-level management are based. Approximately 80% of InMode’s staff work outside of Israel.

Given recent results and news about a potential takeover, investors will want to know the general risk-reward profile of this company. I’ll assess this all below.

Is A Turnaround Imminent?

Turnaround stories can make for excellent investments so long as an investor has priced in the risk and signs of a real catalyst are apparent. I think at this point, for InMode, much of the downside risk has already been priced in – the company has a dirt cheap valuation, and the share price seems to have “settled” at a point where buying and selling among investors has equaled out. But are there any signs of a catalyst for growth ahead? At this point, I’ll give it a solid “maybe.”

For sure, InMode has been struggling with declining revenue growth and now falling revenue for several years, and this has been reflected in its declining market cap (and concomitant share price) over the same period.

While the share price has stagnated for approximately two years (below $20), the company’s significant share repurchases have seemingly done little to boost its market cap, which has continued to fall. However, I do see some green shoots appearing for the company, which would suggest that my previous call of “market bottom” in December might hold up. Shares have been flat since then, but at present, my “strong buy” recommendations have proven poor, or at least—if I’m being very generous with myself—premature. Obviously, many investors will want to see more than just green shoots and instead will want to see real results through several quarters of revenue growth. We are not there, but I will add “yet” to this point, as I do believe the company may have seen the worst of its sentiment, and therefore a reversal could be imminent. Let’s start with its revenue to assess its turnaround potential.

Green Shoot 1: Consumables And Servicing Revenue Growth

Last year the company generated just over $370 million in total revenue. This translated to $84 million in FCF (a 22.75% margin), but was a 36.1% decline from 2024 ($132 million) and more than a 50% reduction from 2023 ($176.1 million). Gross and operating margins contracted meaningfully over this period, as seen below.

InMode generates their revenue through equipment sales—they sell their platforms to physicians (e.g., dermatologists) or trained medi-spa owners—and through consumables and services. The former generated $289 million in 2025, representing 78% of revenue, and the latter generated just over $81 million and the remaining 22%. While equipment sales represent what physicians/owners might perceive to be future demand—they purchase a platform so they can serve what they see as a growing, if not at least stable, market—consumables and servicing revenue is related to past demand, in relation to how many patients are getting treatments done. It also, indirectly, is a forward-looking signal for physicians/owners of general market demand, as they will be generating revenue that aligns with the consumables/servicing revenue that InMode generates. As patients get treatments, the clinic requires consumables and servicing to restock and sustain their business.

This is an important distinction, as consumables and servicing represent recurring revenue for the company and are a good signal of potential future demand in platform sales. Equipment sales come when physicians/owners seek to bolster or update their in-house offerings as a result of what they perceive to be strong (enough) demand to provide a return on their investment. But it is a lagging indicator, to my mind.

So, the first green shoot can be seen in their revenue mix. It is important for investors to note that total full-year 2025 revenue declined by 6%, but for consumables/servicing, actually increased by 2.7%. Moreover, quarterly revenue from both segments saw an increase: 5.7% for equipment and 7.9% for consumables, which suggests the most recent quarterly improvements drove most of this incremental gain. In general, this suggests that consumers are beginning to return to aesthetic treatments after a prolonged period of market softness. CEO Moshe Mizrahy stated of this increase in the Q4 call:

We believe this may represent early sign of stabilization in patient activity and usage level across our installed base. We view 2026 as stabilization year for the business following a prolonged period of industry softness.

In this regard, however, I believe that Mizrahy is mainly referring to the U.S. here, as there has not been so much “industry softness” in Asia or Europe, or at least not as much. And this brings me to my next green shoot.

Green Shoot 2: International Revenue Growth

While the market has become fixated on revenue decline, it is ignoring the nuances of where this decline is coming from and also where—perhaps—revenue is actually growing. This is in relation to market penetration. Should a particular geographical market become saturated with InMode’s platforms, then new machine sales will fall. I believe, from what I’ve read/heard, that this has happened in several places, especially in major U.S. markets like Los Angeles or parts of Florida—I discuss this in my previous article—so InMode’s future revenue-generating potential in those markets will come from new platforms (like Envision) and from consumables/servicing. But they clearly need to do a better job selling these new platforms to their current installed base, and this will be a job for their new President of North American Sales. Certainly, outside of those saturated markets, perhaps overseas, InMode has a much larger addressable market to fill, but this will take time. InMode has dedicated sales teams in several regions overseas to fill this need.

So, another green shoot for the company is their international revenue growth. Last year, U.S. revenue fell from $244.8 million in 2024 to $198.7 million, while outside of the U.S., it increased from $150 million to $172.8 million (14.5%). It stands to reason that as international revenue comes to take a larger proportion of their total revenue, which it most certainly will, this will begin to offset their declines in U.S. revenue. Moreover, the international market is much larger than the US could ever be, so even in the absolute worst-case scenario, the U.S. market is now almost entirely saturated, and InMode can still enjoy probably a decade of growth from overseas as they build out their installed base in Europe and Asia, areas where the growth rate has been strong. In 2019, non-U.S. revenue represented just 21% of InMode’s total sales, but last year it reached 46%.

In my estimation, 2026 or 2027 could be the first year when international (non-U.S.) revenue is larger than U.S. revenue, and this will have a very positive impact on the company’s revenue growth rates. I do not believe the market is paying any attention to this right now, but it’s a major growth driver for the company.

In terms of the general business turnaround idea, investors can be forgiven for taking a cynical view here, as management has hinted at this on previous earnings calls, but the improved consumables/servicing revenue figure and international sales growth provide more than just anecdotal evidence. It is a signal of improving consumer demand, which in the case of the former—if sustained—should lead to improving demand for platform/equipment sales in the U.S., which is their main revenue driver. And with interest rates continuing to come down—albeit slowly—this is also helpful for physicians/owners who are leasing InMode’s technology, usually over a 5-year period.

Overseas, they are firing on all cylinders, and I see a massive market the company can penetrate with their strong international presence. In 2025, they added approximately 10 countries to their sales mix and two new subsidiaries, in Thailand and Argentina.

Finally, as a consequence of what they perceive to be “the bottom,” management has guided for flat revenue growth for 2026 ($365-375 million), which would signal something positive after several years of revenue decline, as seen above.

Within two or three quarters, we should get confirmation of these trends, but for now we only have management’s conservative guidance to go on.

Valuation Considerations Amidst Takeover News

InMode continues to trade at historical lows. It currently offers a FCF yield of 9.3%, which means that – should FCF stabilize as it very much could – the company will earn back its entire market cap in less than 11 years. If it diverted its FCF to buybacks, it could have a material impact on the share price. On this note, the current shareholder yield for the stock is 18.8%, which is fantastic, but Mizrahy has repeatedly voiced frustration at the share price reaction to the buybacks. For example, in the Q1 2025 earnings call, he commented:

Now, actually from an investment point of view of the company, we actually invested $500 million, but it did not help the stock price. The stock price is around $15 today. So basically, it was not the best – the best investment from the corporation point of view, and I'm sure it was not the best investment from the shareholders' point of view.

I noted in my previous article that I felt the CEO was showing some ignorance to the importance of stock buybacks in this commentary, which questioned what value it provided shareholders. Others shared my view. A major shareholder (DOMA Perpetual—owning 4.5% of shares) has repeatedly voiced frustration at the lack of understanding management seems to express as to why share buybacks represent the best interests of shareholders, for example, through public letters like this one.

As a publicly traded company, management has an obligation to use the cash on its balance sheet to return value to shareholders, either by growing the business (M&A, organic growth, etc.) and/or rewarding shareholders. While dividends have been considered as an option here, the deflated share price currently suggests that buybacks offer the best return in this regard, yet management has questioned this capital allocation strategy previously, despite having bought back more than $500 million worth of shares over previous quarters. This remains a major frustration for me as a shareholder—to be clear, not the buybacks but management’s short-term focus on share-price reaction to the buybacks—and it has led many like me to question management’s competence.

In a more recent open letter from DOMA Perpetual, the investment fund manager Pedro Escudero called for the InMode board to remove Mizrahy from his position. Another letter several months later reiterated their frustration and called for still further buybacks to maximize shareholder value (20% of company shares over the following two years).

Mizrahy responded to this “investor activism” by claiming, recently:

Two years ago they (DOMA and others) started driving the management and board crazy … They started saying that the company was incorrectly managed, that I as a manager wasn’t doing what they told me to do. They wanted the company to carry out a large buyback of its shares. We had $1 billion cash, earmarked for a significant acquisition, and they threatened legal proceedings … They persuaded the board to approve the share buyback. If all that hadn’t happened, we would probably have acquired a large complementary business in the filler materials – hyaluronic acids and botoxes – something with innovative technology which would have added to the bottom line immediately.

While I certainly appreciate the frankness of management to voice their feelings openly, I also found this response troubling and, not least, a little bit confusing. Indeed, in previous communications, Mizrahy expressed discontent at the lack of investment opportunities in this space. Now he is saying that the company had a billion dollars earmarked for an acquisition. Well, which is it?

In the Q4 2024 earnings call, when asked about M&A opportunities in the market, he responded:

Well, as you know, we are always exploring M&A opportunities. Once it's come, we will report that we did. So far, we are not able to find something that we can buy or very synergetic to us … Again, if opportunity will present itself which will be either complementary to our product line or synergetic to our product line or something that had to do with the aesthetic that we do not make, I mean in the energy-based devices, which are similar to what we are doing, we will explore it and report. But right now, we have nothing in the pipeline.

In the Q2 2025 call, he reiterated this point:

We don't have any acquisition on the pipeline. So I cannot announce that we're doing any acquisition this year.

As such, Mizrahy’s more recent comments in this regard strike me as being a bit opportunistic, seemingly leveraging his frustration at activist investors to deflect criticism of the company’s poor capital allocation strategy. The gist is, "If they hadn’t forced us to buy back our shares, we’d have purchased another company by now and be seeing more revenue." By the sounds of it, when analyzing his commentary on previous calls, however, this does not seem to be true.

In the light of all of this, it is perhaps no wonder that the company has sought a buyout, as revealed approximately three weeks ago in the Israeli news source Calcalist (note: this will need to be translated to English). In an interview for another Israeli online publication some days later, Globes, Mizrahy rationalized this decision to seek a buyer, evidently built from frustration:

It takes time but in the end it penetrates. The desire to go back to being a privately-held company arises from, among other things, the desire to get them (activist investors) off our backs. Running the company has become unpleasant.

Let’s turn to these acquisition rumors now.

Acquisition Rumours

On January 26th, Calcalist suggested that InMode was in talks to be acquired by a private equity firm, valuing the company at $1.1 billion (a ~25% premium on the share price at the time). Several months prior, InMode apparently hired Bank of America (BAC) to find a buyer for the company. Two offers ended up on the table: a Korean-based firm that may have intended to move its headquarters away from Israel and an Israeli-backed one—which includes Mizrahy himself—that would promise to keep the company in Israel. Later, another firm—a minority shareholder (owning 1.3% of the company shares) called Steel Partners – offered to buyout 51% of the company, also valuing it at $1.1 billion.

The news sent shares of INMD up around 19% over the coming days, but they have since fallen back to their previous level after the CEO’s muted response. In the latest earnings call, he bluntly responded to the analyst question on the topic:

I want to comment one thing about the news that Steel Partners released to the market in the press release that they are willing to buy 51% of the company for $18 per share. So I wonder why they sent this letter to me as the CEO and to the Board of Directors. We do not have 51% of the company to sell. So the only way to buy 51% of InMode is to do a tender offer, hire a bank, put some money in an escrow account and offer it to the public, not to the CEO. I don't have 51% to sell and give them. But they didn't do it. They just sent a letter to me and to the Board of Directors. And later, 1 day after, they published it as a press release. Other than that, we have no contact with them whatsoever, not myself, not the Board. We did not talk to them. We did not discuss it with them. We don't know why they put the press release out.

From the perspective of Steel Partners, I suspect this was a lowball offer to gauge InMode’s desperation, rather than a serious pitch. As such, it likely puts these rumors to bed for the time being, but in any event, I do not believe that investors would have agreed to the sale at that price anyway, as it values the company (minus their cash and equivalents of $555 million) at just $550 million. Considering they generated $84 million in FCF in the last 12 months, this seems to grossly undervalue their IP, brand, and mix of other assets. As such, the chances of this getting approved by shareholders would have been low, in my opinion.

An offer over $2 billion might be more warmly received by shareholders, but as yet, there has not been any official response beyond this one, which is not particularly helpful in terms of gauging management’s views. Further, I can find no information on Steel Partners website that even mentions the offer—no news source or press release on it, for example—which attests to its speculative nature. This is my personal opinion on the matter, and I’d be willing to adjust it if either company offered something more concrete to go on.

For now, investors should focus on what we know for sure about InMode: its products, markets, and revenues. I’ll turn now to recent developments before assessing the company’s valuation.

Expanded Product Offerings

Morpheus8 continues to be the company’s flagship offering in the minimally invasive space. To date, there have been a reported 3.5 million Morpheus8 treatments worldwide—one every 7.3 seconds (8.2 every minute)—and the product line has won multiple awards.

Minimally-invasive treatments like Morpheus8 represent 76% of their revenue in terms of platform and consumables sales, while non-invasive represents 23% and the remaining 1% is for their newest segment: hands-free. They now offer a dozen different platforms, with more in production. I suggest they are the strongest player in this niche space and a market leader.

Most recently, InMode secured approval in Canada for Envision, which offers ocular treatments. It is approved by Health Canada to treat the symptoms of dry eye disease caused by Melbomian Gland Dysfunction. This is a condition that affects around 70% of people over 60 years of age, with those wearing contact lenses particularly at risk. The InMode treatment Forma-I uses RF technology to heat the eyelids, melt the gland blockage, and improve tear lubrication, thus alleviating symptoms.

They are in the process of conducting a research study that should underpin future FDA approval. In the latest quarterly earnings call, Mizrahy highlighted that they had put together a specialized sales team to sell the Envision product in North America. This makes sense given that sales reps will be talking to a different population of physicians and must therefore have a different knowledge base in comparison to their core products that focus on body/face contouring and subdermal adipose remodeling through collagen production.

Like many of their other platforms, Envision is not only used or intended for just one treatment but several, with a combination often getting the best results from a patient’s perspective. The machine can also perform Morpheus8 and Lumecca-1 (using intense pulsed light treatment for removing age spots, sun damage, vascular lesions, rosacea, and freckles) for patients around the eyes.

Management also spent time in the call highlighting their efforts in the hands-free space, of which EvolveX is their latest proprietary technology platform.

This type of system offers several advantages for the clinician and patient. The EvolveX platform itself supports multiple technologies (as seen in the image above) and can be used across different areas of the body, which brings certain operational and cost efficiencies.

Finally, in the latest call, management discussed their new treatments. After launching their CO2 product, purchased from a small US-based company under FDA clearance, they plan to launch two new products in 2026. The first is a combination therapy adding Erbium:YAG (a superficial laser treatment used for skin resurfacing/texturing) to their Morpheus8 product. They are also launching a Pico laser, which they purchased from a small Korean company, and QMode, a Q-switched laser for pigmentation, helping to diminish the appearance of spots, freckles, and sun damage, and treat melasma, post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation, and tattoo removal. The company acknowledged that there is competition in this space and that the introduction of these laser products will impact their gross margins—with lower selling prices and import tariffs—but that it will help create synergies with their other products and also create cross-selling opportunities.

As a medical aesthetics company, it makes sense for InMode to offer a product line for laser treatments, which are cheaper for the patient. This will help their brand awareness and generate interest for their more expensive treatments should patients feel the need for more effective results.

All of these developments are funded through R&D spending, which was about $13.3 million in 2025, similar to the previous 2 years. Management has noted their intention to bring out two new proprietary technologies each year, which I applaud. Again, while cynical investors will be quick to point to declining revenue, these investments do at least suggest that management is looking toward the future with its long-term IP and platform development. Investors should remember this. Let’s turn to its financials and assess the broad risk-reward of the company with shares hovering between $14 and $15 (P/E of 10) at present.

Financial Metrics

As mentioned above, InMode has cash and equivalents of $555 million, representing 60% of their current market cap. Even including this massive cash balance, the company still trades at a PE of just 10 and a forward P/E of 9.9 (signaling flat earnings growth for 2026), a PS ratio of 2.5, and a P/B ratio of 1.3.

Their balance sheet is in excellent shape. They have minimal debt, a debt/equity ratio of 0.01, a current ratio of 9.88, and a quick ratio of 8.55. Obviously, while their large cash balance provides lots of protection, investors will recognize that the cash is not providing them a return on their investment, as it perhaps should. That said, the company still boasts decent returns from equity, assets and invested capital.

These numbers are still very good and signal the enormous potential for the company’s share price if—and it is an "if" until they can prove it—management can show shareholders they have turned the ship around.

Conclusion

While market sentiment for InMode remains very poor, I have spotted two green shoots in their latest full-year results, namely:

  • Strong international growth, whereby in 2026 or 2027, non-U.S. revenue could eclipse U.S. revenue for the first time and offset what appears to be an increasingly saturated market in the U.S.
  • An improvement in consumables and servicing revenue, which is an early indicator of market growth among patients seeking out these treatments, and a suggestion of a modest equipment sales ramp ahead.

If we add to this their new product lines, their moves into laser treatments, their fantastic balance sheet that can be leveraged to create shareholder value, and their historically low valuation, I feel that the risk-reward is tilted very favorably for investors right now. Shares have a very strong base of support around $14, with plenty of upside potential ahead if management’s assessment of 2026 representing the “bottom” is to be believed.

My patience with management has been severely tested, but I do believe they understand the market very well, and they have the technology, brand, and distribution networks to reignite growth internationally and in the U.S. with their new product lines. Risk-averse investors may wish to see a couple of quarters of revenue growth ahead before investing here, but those who are happy to front-run recovery stories like this would be unlikely to find a better entry point, in my view. I have been heavily buying in the $14 range for several months now, and I continue to view shares as a Strong Buy, despite my reservations with management. I believe shares hitting $18-20 before the end of 2026 would not be an unreasonable projection.

data.title: Coherent: A Compelling Opportunity For Patient Investors
data.description:
I have written about several optical/laser companies over the last several months, including Lumentum Holdings (LITE) at the end of December and Ciena (CIEN) on February 1. This article will be my first look at Coherent (COHR). Although Coherent has done well over the last year, up 152.9%, it has not done nearly as well as its most similar competitor, Lumentum, which is up 571.9%.

Lumentum's emphasis on optical equipment sales to Artificial Intelligence ("AI") and cloud companies has driven accelerating revenue growth. It has also handily beaten analysts' quarterly expectations over the last year.

On the other hand, although Coherent has deep exposure to the Artificial Intelligence ("AI") optical market, it is less focused than Lumentum. Coherent also manufactures lasers for industrial manufacturing (cutting, welding, and engraving), medical applications (LASIK eye surgery and similar dermatology lasers for skin conditions), aerospace (3D mapping via LiDAR and optical space communication), and semiconductor manufacturing equipment. The company also develops 3D sensing components for smartphones and advanced optical sensors. Coherent is a more extensive, more diversified "generalist" laser and photonics company with more growth drivers than AI alone can provide.

Coherent's diversification has several advantages: it is slightly less exposed to potential cyclicalities arising from inventory corrections or hyperscaler AI spending cycles. Additionally, it has significant exposure to several markets that may also eventually experience outsize growth, including Silicon Carbide (SiC) power electronics (used in Electric Vehicles), automotive LiDAR, Augmented Reality ("AR")/Virtual Reality ("VR") displays, industrial laser automation, quantum optical technologies, and advanced sensing.

However, AI optics is currently booming, and Coherent's slightly lower exposure to AI optics than Lumentum shows up in the financial results. Lumentum's second quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2026 year-over-year revenue growth was 65%, compared to Coherent's 17% year-over-year growth.

AI optics also has higher margins, and Lumentum's greater exposure to AI optics may be reflected in its higher gross margins. Coherent's second quarter non-GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) gross margin was 39%, 3.5 points lower than Lumentum's second quarter non-GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) gross margin of 42.5%.

Additionally, although Coherent has beaten analysts' revenue and earnings estimates over the last year, bullish investors haven't always been satisfied with management's forward guidance. For example, in the fourth-quarter 2025 results, management provided first-quarter FY 2026 revenue guidance with a midpoint of $1.53 billion, less than analysts' estimates of $1.55 billion. Although the market was fully aware that the revenue guidance miss resulted from the divestiture of its Aerospace and Defense business unit, the stock declined 19.61% on August 14, 2025, the day after the fourth-quarter earnings release. The market may have been underwhelmed by the guidance relative to Lumentum's.

Even when Coherent beat analyst revenue, earnings, and guidance, investors haven't always been satisfied. For instance, the stock declined by 21% from the February 4 closing price of $227.68 to the February 5 market opening price of $179.11. The stock rose throughout the day as investors refocused on its long-term upside potential, and the stock closed approximately 1% below the February 4 close. The company's valuation is extraordinarily sensitive to its monetary performance.

Although Lumentum's recent financial track record relative to Coherent makes it appear a better bet, there are reasons to consider investing in Coherent either alongside Lumentum or instead of Lumentum. Coherent has several advantages that the market expects to begin to pay off in 2027.

Analysts expect FY 2027 revenue to increase to 24.17% from 19.06% in FY 2026. One reason for analysts' confidence in a strong Coherent FY 2027 is rising demand for its data center products and the expectation for accelerating revenue growth over the next several quarters. Chief Executive ("CEO") James Anderson said on the second quarter FY 2026 earnings call:

As a result of the AI build-out, we saw strong revenue and profit growth in our December quarter. We also experienced another step function increase in our bookings, which we expect to increase again in our current quarter. Given the extraordinary strength and visibility of demand from our customers, combined with our continued rapid expansion and production capacity, we expect a period of sustained strong revenue growth over the coming quarters. In particular, we expect continued strong sequential revenue growth in both our March and June quarters, and we expect our fiscal '27 revenue growth rate to exceed our fiscal '26 growth rate.

Although Coherent has less exposure to AI than Lumentum, it still benefits greatly from AI data center growth. Much of the company's revenue growth is powered by sales of lasers for its 800 G and 1.6 T transceivers and Optical Circuit Switch ("OCS"). CEO Anderson added the following on the earnings call, pointing out the company's competitive advantages:

Data center's...book-to-bill ratio...exceeded 4x as customer demand continues to increase and customers place orders further out in time, which provides us with strong visibility for the coming quarters. The strength of our product portfolio, combined with our vertical integration and our expanding U.S. manufacturing footprint, provide a clear competitive advantage with our customers.

Vertical integration means that Coherent controls its product lifecycle from raw materials through finished subsystems, conceptually enabling it to produce laser chips at lower cost over time. Theoretically, Coherent should achieve higher gross margins than its competitors. The company also exhibits operating leverage, meaning operating income should grow faster than revenue. An example is the company's second-quarter GAAP operating income, which grew 34.40%, faster than revenue, which grew 17.48%. Investors should monitor future quarters to see if the company can maintain operating leverage.

Although Lumentum's stock has recently outperformed Coherent's, this may not always be the case. If Coherent's structural advantages translate into financial advantages relative to competitors, it could outperform the Laser Systems, Components, and Materials industry, including Lumentum, in the future.

Let us examine its primary method for controlling manufacturing costs: increasing the size of the laser chip wafer.

Increasing Wafer Size From 3-inch to 6-inch

If you are unfamiliar with lasers, you might assume, as I did, that lasers used for fiber optics are similar to traditional gas-tube lasers used for cutting and engraving. Industrial lasers work by creating a high-voltage electrical discharge in a gaseous mixture, such as CO₂ or Helium-Neon, which produces photons. Using mirrors, these photons are focused into a laser beam.

While industrial lasers use gases to generate photons, fiber-optic lasers use a solid-state semiconductor made of a material such as Indium Phosphide (InP) to generate photons. When electricity is applied to a specific region of a semiconductor material called the active region, it releases energy in the form of a photon.

InP emits infrared photons, the ideal wavelength range for propagation through fiber-optic glass with low signal loss. As a result, you will see InP primarily used in high-performance optical lasers for long-haul telecommunications, data center interconnects, and sensing technologies, namely LIDAR. Semiconductor lasers are also thousands of times smaller, faster, and more efficient than industrial lasers. Photonics Manufacturers can also produce many more lasers operating at the microscopic scale than those manufacturers producing gas-tube lasers for large-scale uses.

Coherent's vertical integration advantages are evident in InP lasers. Laser manufacturers that lack vertical integration purchase InP wafers from other companies, such as Sumitomo Electric, AXT, Inc., and InPACT.

In contrast, Coherent buys high‑purity Indium and other elemental precursors to grow its own InP epitaxial structures and converts them into wafer substrates. By controlling the InP wafer manufacturing process, the company avoids the extra fees third-party InP wafer producers add to the final product, improves the number of chips a wafer can produce, and reduces material costs, factors that can directly support higher gross margins relative to competitors who rely on third-party suppliers of finished InP wafers.

We will now discuss how Coherent is improving the number of usable chips per wafer relative to the total number of possible chips on a wafer, a metric referred to as yield. On the company's most recent earnings call, CEO Anderson discussed the benefits of increasing the size of the InP wafer from three inches to six inches: "As a reminder, our indium phosphide capacity expansion is driven by a ramp of 6-inch wafer production; a 6-inch wafer compared to a 3-inch wafer will produce more than 4x as many chips at less than half the cost."

Making wafers larger brings several benefits, including more usable chips per wafer because of the way chips fit on a round surface. Since chips are rectangular and wafers are round, some space at the edges is always wasted. To keep it simple, Coherent can fit many more usable chips on a 6-inch wafer than on a 3-inch one. Also, since labor, electricity, and cleanroom costs are the same for both sizes, making more chips on a larger wafer lowers the cost per chip. The company can also more easily automate the production of 6-inch wafers, further reducing costs. According to a 2024 Coherent blog, there is a "60% reduction in die costs for devices widely used in a broad range of applications." A die is the material cut from a wafer that contains a functional integrated circuit.

The benefits of vertical integration and increasing wafer size are already evident in improved margins. Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") Sherri Luther said on the second quarter FY 2026 earnings call:

Our Q2 non-GAAP gross margin was 39%, a 24 basis point improvement compared to the prior quarter and a 77 basis point improvement as compared to the year ago quarter. We continue to execute on our gross margin expansion strategy, where we generated sequential and year-over-year increases in gross margin, primarily in the Datacenter & Communications segment. These improvements were driven by reductions in product input costs, efficiency gains from improved cycle times in the manufacturing process as well as yield improvements. Pricing optimization also continued to contribute meaningfully to our gross margin expansion.

Lastly, the transition from 3-inch to 6-inch should increase Coherent's production capacity, which should help boost revenue growth over the next few years. CEO Anderson said, "We expect both fiscal '26 and fiscal '27 to be strong growth years for Coherent given our exceptional demand from our customers and the rapid expansion of our production capacity."

Coherent's capacity for increased revenue growth and its possible cost advantage over competing laser manufacturers may show up in the stock's outperformance over the next few years.

Coherent's Financial Condition

Coherent beat second quarter fiscal year ("FY") analysts' revenue estimates by 2.74% and earnings-per-share ("EPS") estimates by 6.6%.

Coherent closed the sale of its Munich-based Rofin brand to Bystronic in late January. The Rofin Brand specializes in industrial gas-tube CO2 lasers, although it also manufactures solid-state and fiber lasers. Coherent sold the brand to refocus the business more toward lasers for telecommunications and AI data centers and away from manufacturing finished industrial lasers, which Rofin Brand focuses on. Coherent will use the proceeds of the sale to repay debt, giving it financial agility and helping it to increase investment in InP solid-state lasers. In the short term, annual revenue will decline by approximately $100 million, or $25 million per quarter, but the company's margins and growth profile should improve. CEO Anderson said on the earnings call, "The sale of this [Munich] product division is expected to be immediately accretive to both gross margin and EPS."

Management's third-quarter FY 2026 revenue guidance midpoint of $1.77 billion topped analysts' third-quarter revenue estimates of $1.70 billion. Following the company's second-quarter earnings release, analysts raised their estimates to match management's guidance of $1.77 billion.

Management's third-quarter FY 2026 non-GAAP EPS guidance midpoint was $1.38, which beat the analysts' consensus estimate of $1.32. Following the company's second-quarter earnings release, analysts raised their EPS estimates to $1.39, which was $0.01 above management's guidance.

Although the company's revenue and EPS outperformed, some investors are dissatisfied with Coherent's operating margin expectations, which are lower than Lumentum's. Coherent doesn't explicitly forecast operating margin. Still, investors can use revenue estimates, non-GAAP gross margins, and non-GAAP operating expenses ("OpEx") to calculate Coherent's implied non-GAAP operating margin of 20.9% at the midpoint of guidance.

In contrast, Lumentum predicts its third quarter FY 2026 non-GAAP operating margin to rise from 25.2% in the second quarter to 30.5% at the midpoint of guidance. Management gave an optimistic margin forecast for three reasons: first, the company has operating leverage (meaning operating profits will grow faster than revenue), and management forecast revenue growth of 89% at the midpoint of guidance. Second, they expect a favorable shift toward a mix of higher-margin products. Third, the company expects better manufacturing operational efficiency through improving chip yields and better capacity utilization of its fabs.

Lumentum's ~30% non-GAAP operating margin forecast implies that the company may be moving beyond the heavy AI investment phase and toward the harvesting phase. In contrast, Coherent is still in the middle of a massive Capital Expenditure ("CapEx") cycle to fund expanding production capacity by building 6-inch wafers.

Although CapEx (balance sheet) and OpEx (income statement) are in different accounting buckets, when a company increases CapEx, OpEx eventually also increases as a delayed effect for several reasons. The most direct reason is that when a company invests in new photolithography equipment and laser etching machines, it must eventually depreciate them over their useful lives (usually five to seven years). Depreciation, a non-cash expense, is sometimes recorded on the income statement as Cost of Goods Sold and sometimes as OpEx, both of which reduce the operating margin.

Additionally, increasing CapEx can cause numerous indirect operating expenses. For instance, new 6-inch wafer fabs require hiring engineers to operate the expensive equipment, paying for utilities (electricity and water), acquiring spare parts, performing maintenance, and absorbing costs associated with defective chips (yield loss). All of these costs are recorded as OpEx. As OpEx rises, operating margins decline.

When investors compare these two companies today, they see one company whose investments are paying off today (Lumentum) and another where it may take several more years for the investments to pay off (Coherent). Generally, the market assigns a higher value to a company that yields a higher return today than to one forecast to yield a higher return down the road.

Earlier in this article, I stated, "The company's valuation is extraordinarily sensitive to its monetary performance." If the market has priced in a financial showing that Coherent fails to meet, despite "beating analysts' expectations," investors may re-rate the valuation lower, which was a major reason for the initial 19% drop on the February 5 open that I mentioned earlier in this article.

The company's cash and restricted cash are $899.19 million. Its long-term debt is 3.2454 billion (From its balance sheet). The long-term debt listed on YCharts is slightly off because sometimes third-party data providers include other reported figures in their definition of long-term debt. I included YCharts' long-term debt data because it has historically been directionally accurate.

The company's debt-to-equity ratio is 0.4, indicating that Coherent conservatively finances its operations with more equity than debt.

If you look up this company's net debt-to-EBITDA (Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization) on different third-party websites, you will see different figures. Many third-party websites report Coherent's net debt-to-EBITDA ratio as 2.8. However, a calculated net debt-to-EBITDA ratio based on Seeking Alpha's figures (as of February 10) is 2.21, using net debt of $2,675.8 million and TTM EBITDA of $1,208.2 million. There are several reasons why different websites may report different figures.

Disparate financial websites may define net debt differently. For instance, some may use the standard definition of total debt (short-term plus long-term) minus cash and cash equivalents. Others may use adjusted net debt, which may include lease liabilities, restricted cash adjustments, pension obligations, exclude cash held for M&A/escrow, exclude short‑term investments, or treat marketable securities differently. Similarly, different websites calculate EBITDA differently.

Generally, when I analyze a company, I use the debt-to-EBITDA figures it provides because the company likely uses lenders' covenant definitions for net debt and EBITDA, meaning the company's earnings report refers to figures lenders use to assess its debt. CFO Luther stated on the earnings call: "We maintained our debt leverage ratio at 1.7x, down from 2.3x in the year-ago quarter," which shows the company is deleveraging. Coherent either reduced its debt, increased its EBITDA, or did both. At a debt leverage ratio of 1.7x, it has room to fund Capital Expenditure ("CapEx"), ramp production, or pursue small acquisitions without instant pressure to raise equity or reduce investment.

The company's Datacenter & Communications segment, which now accounts for over 70% of its revenue, generates most of Coherent's revenue growth. Most of that segment's growth comes from 800G optical transceivers, and the company has just begun production of next-generation 1.6T optical transceivers. The tremendous demand from data centers and communication providers for 800G and the new 1.6T optical transceivers has created an InP supply-demand imbalance, straining the whole laser industry's ability to create enough InP wafers to keep up with demand. CEO Anderson addressed the InP supply-demand situation on the second-quarter earnings call:

It seems like every quarter, we think we're going to catch up and then the [InP] demand keeps increasing. So I don't foresee the supply/demand getting back in balance this calendar year. I don't think it happens next calendar year. And if the forecast that we're seeing from customers for indium phosphide laser supply that they need for scale up, I think we could be in a very sustained long period of supply-demand imbalance on indium phosphide, which is exactly why we're super focused on ramping our 6-inch capacity as quickly as possible. That near-term goal is to double our capacity by the end of this year, but we're driving goals beyond that, that are very aggressive in terms of our continued ramp of indium phosphide capacity. And the demand that we're seeing from the customers absorbs all of that capacity and then some.

When Coherent accelerates efforts to "double" InP wafer capacity by the end of 2026, it will require fab expansion, procurement of precursor InP materials, and the creation of an inventory buildup of InP wafers, all of which will consume substantial cash. Many things can suppress Cash From Operations ("CFO"). Still, it is likely that a significant portion of Coherent's second-quarter FY 2026 69% decline in cash from operations reflects efforts to increase InP supply.

As a result of the second quarter of FY 2026, cash from operations declined to $57.90 million, and CapEx increased to $153.60 million. The company incurred $95.70 million in Free Cash Flow ("FCF") losses in the second quarter, which is something some investors will balk at.

Even if the company raises non-GAAP operating margins to the high 20s or low 30s, investors may not increase the stock's valuation until it returns to FCF-positive status.

Valuation

Coherent's trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) is 6.230, significantly above its three-, five-, seven-, and ten-year medians. The market either overvalues the stock or expects accelerating revenue growth, propelled by its brightening prospects in AI optics. However, if the company fails to achieve the expected revenue growth, the stock could drop. I am not sure whether its valuation will drop to its three-year median, as that was the level before AI began to have a significant impact on revenue. However, if the company misses revenue targets, the stock will likely have a significant selloff. Coherent's three-year median corresponds to a stock price of $75.34, a 67% decline from its February 10 closing price of $228.37.

The following table compares Coherent's two-year forward P/S ratio with its estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) and TTM profitability, as well as those of several peers in the optical components and lasers industry. Generally, investors consider stocks with the highest P/S or P/S/G ratio to be overvalued.

Company Name FY 2027 forward P/S ratio FY 2027 estimated forward revenue growth FY 2027 forward P/S ratio-to-estimated revenue growth (P/S/G) TTM FCF margin TTM Gross Margin TTM Operating Margin TTM ROIC
Coherent 4.97 24.17% 0.21 -1.6% 36.2% 8.1% 4%
Lumentum Holdings 10.51 55.52% 0.19 -1.4% 33.4% 1.2% 4.1%
IPG Photonics (IPGP) 4.24 8.04% 0.53 3.8% 38.7% 2.5% 0.9%
Viavi Solutions (VIAV) 3.77 10.64% 0.35 6.3% 56.6% 3.4% 4.5%
Fabrinet (FN) 3.59 15.31% 0.23 2.6% 12% 9.7% 48.1%
Marvell (MRVL) 6.89 22.19% 0.31 20.3% 50.7% 14.8% 5.9%
Ciena (CIEN) 5.65 16.41% 0.34 13.9% 42% 4.1% 4.4%
Applied Optoelectronics (AAOI) 3.96 68.40% 0.06 -71.6% 29.3% -11.8% -10.4%
Median 4.24 16.41% 0.31 3.8% 38.7% 3.4% 4.4%

Source: Forward revenue growth rates come from Seeking Alpha. TTM profitability comes from a third-party website.

On the one hand, some may consider Coherent's two-year forward P/S ratio of 4.97 to be overvalued relative to the peer median of 4.24. On the other hand, the peer group's median P/S/G is 0.31, higher than Coherent's P/S/G of 0.21, which some investors may consider a sign of undervaluation based solely on P/S/G.

Things become more muddled when business profitability measures are included in the equation. First, be aware that I am using trailing 12-month ("TTM") GAAP profitability and not quarterly numbers in the comparison. TTM metrics may not completely capture the company's performance in its recent quarter. Additionally, elsewhere in this article, I discuss Coherent and Lumentum's non-GAAP operating margins. So, be careful not to directly compare GAAP to non-GAAP or TTM to forward-looking profitability measures.

Coherent's FCF and gross margins are below the medians. Its operating margin exceeds the median of its peers. The company's ROIC is below the median. Its profitability measures indicate a mixed picture.

Given that future profitability could improve from higher yields on 6‑inch wafers, new capacity coming online, and vertical integration, the stock appears fairly valued, with potential upside if the company's strategies result in it outperforming analysts' revenue and earnings-per-share ("EPS") estimates in future quarters.

The company's TTM price-to-earnings (P/E) is 181.45, well above the Information Technology sector median of 31.26. Investors may interpret this as indicating that the market overvalues the stock or has already priced in accelerating EPS growth.

Coherent's FY 2026 forward P/E to analysts' estimated EPS growth ("PEG") ratio is 0.82, calculated by dividing its forward P/E of 42.60 by its estimated EPS growth of 51.85%. Generally, a PEG ratio below 1.0 is considered indicative of undervaluation.

Suppose the stock sells at its FY 2026 PEG ratio of 1.0 (a sign of fair value); the stock price would be $277.91, a 22% increase over its February 10 closing price.

Generally, the market will allow a growth stock's PEG ratio to reach 2.0. However, PEG ratios of 2.0 and higher are generally reserved for software or cloud growth companies.

Coherent is a hardware manufacturer. Even with the tailwinds of AI, the market rarely, if ever, values a fiber optics or networking company at a forward PEG ratio of 2.0. The maximum forward PEG ratio that the market might be willing to assign to a company like Coherent is 1.50, considering that the market currently values Ciena at a FY 2027 forward PEG ratio of 1.52 (Ciena forward P/E of 44.13 divided by its EPS growth rate of 28.95)

If Coherent reaches a 2026 forward PEG ratio of 1.50, the stock price would be $416.87, an 82.5% rise above its February 10 closing price. If it sold at its FY 2027 PEG ratio of 1.5, its price would be $388.43, which is 70% above its February 10 closing price.

YChart shows that the company's TTM price-to-FCF (P/FCF) is 418.65, which I had to verify since the company currently has a TTM FCF of negative $103.62 million, and negative FCF can't produce a positive P/FCF. One explanation is that YChart's figure may use a different TTM window or FCF definition. Coherent's P/FCF is either negative or barely positive; therefore, the P/FCF valuation metric is unreliable for this stock.

Some bullish investors will view these numbers through the lens of short-term pain for long-term gain. They view a negative or extremely high P/FCF ratio as a short-term issue to be tolerated to realize anticipated long-term gains in 2027, when the new InP capacity comes online, and FCF is expected to normalize.

More risk-averse investors are concerned that the company's risks could delay its return to positive FCF, prompting the market to re-rate the stock downward.

Coherent currently has an Enterprise Value ("EV") to EBITDA ratio of 26.56.

Analysts estimate that Coherent will grow its EBITDA at a compound annual growth rate of 30.2%, from $1.64 billion to $2.78 billion over the next two years.

Let us compare these figures with several of its peers. I included this forward EV/EBITDA comparison to present a valuation metric that doesn’t exhibit the same adverse effects as P/E and P/FCF during periods of heavy investment, because EV/EBITDA accounts for Coherent's debt paydown and the operating leverage it gains as new 6-inch wafer fabs ramp up production. The following comparison is an effective means of evaluating companies at different stages of the AI cycle.

In the following comparison, investors generally consider companies undervalued when their forward EV/EBITDA or growth-adjusted forward EV/EBITDA trades below the peer group median.

Company Name Forward EV/EBITDA Two-year EBITDA CAGR Forward EV/EBITDA/growth TTM EBITDA Margin
Applied Optoelectronics 47.51 55.42% 0.86 -6.10
Lumentum 47.35 55.2% 0.86 12.33%
IPG Photonics 37.07 27.35% 1.36 9.62%
Ciena 36.72 22.29% 1.64 9.46%
Fabrinet 29.94 18.2% 1.65 11.27%
Coherent 26.56 30.2% 0.88 19.94%
Viavi Solution 21.20 16.59% 1.28 12.99%
Marvell 19.47 15.39% 1.27 31.39%
Median 36.72 22.29% 1.28 11.27%

Since Coherent has both a forward EV/EBITDA and a Forward EV/EBITDA/growth below the median, investors may consider it undervalued, particularly given that its EBITDA margin is above the median. If the company's strategic plans translate to increased EBITDA growth in line with analysts' expectations, the market likely undervalues Coherent.

However, be aware that this analysis comes with risks. If Coherent is unable to deliver finished InP products, such as transceivers, on the planned timelines, the company may fail to meet analysts' EBITDA growth targets, and investors may begin considering the stock overvalued.

Risks

On the positive side, the CEO said on the second-quarter earnings call, "In terms of wafer starts, we're already at 80% of that goal to double our capacity this [calendar] year." However, the key to doubling capacity by the end of the calendar year is to produce enough 6-inch wafers with a yield higher than that of 3-inch wafers. Coherent has achieved 6-inch wafer yields exceeding those of 3-inch wafers on initial runs at its Sherman, Texas, plant. CEO Anderson stated on the first-quarter FY 2026 earnings call, "I am very pleased to share that our initial 6-inch indium phosphide production yields are actually higher than our current 3-inch indium phosphide yields."

The company has also begun ramping up 6-inch production at a second InP plant in Jarfalla, Sweden, following the success achieved at its first plant. Still, success at one plant doesn't guarantee success at another plant. For instance, if Jarfalla operators fail to calibrate the equipment or environmental controls correctly, or operators at the site lack the expertise to operate the equipment, the yield at its second InP plant might temporarily be lower than expected and slow the overall ramp-up of InP supply. If the Jarfalla plant experiences issues, the market would likely not take the news well.

To complicate matters, Coherent is not just making wafers for one product, but for three: Electro-absorption Modulated Lasers (EML), Continuous-Wave ("CW") lasers, and photodiodes. Each product requires different specifications for the InP wafers (doping type, purity, crystal orientation, and mechanical finish). If the company changes the wafer type it manufactures in reaction to market demand for a specific product and subsequently changes the wafer specifications, it may be unable to sustain the same yield. Another factor is that, as the company transitions from initial small-batch production to full-scale production, it may be unable to preserve the same yield after adjusting various settings. There is some execution risk as Coherent ramps up InP production for various products. Although everything is going well now, there is no guarantee that the company won't encounter obstacles to doubling its InP production.

Another consideration is that the company must turn a wafer into a finished product to generate revenue. CEO Anderson also said on the earnings call, "Typical time from a wafer start to, like, a transceiver shipment is about 6 months." Until now, I have primarily discussed wafer production. However, to get a useful end product like a transceiver, the company must fabricate the desired chips on the wafer, test the chips while on the wafer, dice or cut the wafer into individual chips, retest the chips after dicing, and send them from the Texas or Swedish fab to Malaysia or Vietnam for mounting in a finished package.
